text: With any system there are startup costs. The startup costs for 3D printing are amongst the cheapest going. The only continuing cost is the resin or filament.  Hardware is all you have to pay for. The software is all free: Free CAD, Tinker CAD, Cura, Mesh Mixer, GIMP, Blender, Thiniverse. And you can learn how to do everything on YouTube. If you really want to do it, find away.

Two quickies. I bought my frst 3D printer as a kit (Smartt) four years ago. It was a test to put it together and get it to run smoothly but the experience was a learning curve and gave me a good understanding of the 3D printing process just by building the machine. Like you, I bought a Vigo Tec laser cutter (£140). Sure, not a state of the art machine but it does the job. I don't own a £X0,000 car that is rusting on the drivaeway costing £X00 per week in depreciation, so whats all the gripes from followers about a 3D printer or cheap laser cutter costs - costing less than a week's car depreciation. Look after the pennies and . . . .
Keep up yhour great work and inspirational videos. 
Glad to see you back to full steam following your sad loss earlier this year. Commiserations

author: @crazymonkeyVII
text:
I agree with all your points. Regarding the material cost: A friend of mine has a 3D printer and has some equipment with it that allows him to recycle many plastic into new plastic wire for his printer. He's on a very low budget so I don't think that's an expensive acquisition (although it does really require you to learn about different types of plastics and associated safety risks, but then again you should already know that stuff if you want to use the printer itself in your home).

The only two things I would argue against using 3D printing for everything are: 
1) There's something to be said for gaining skills with manual construction methods. I think you get more experience and technical insight from learning how to make it by hand. You're spending so much time on individual components that you will put a lot more thought and effort into them. The flip side to that argument is, that most people don't have a whole lot of time to make things and 3D printing can save a lot of time, thus 3D printing allows more people to try and create (new) things.
2) 3D printed materials are often more fragile than hand-made or machine-made counterparts made from other materials. This is of course inherent to them being made from plastic. In a lot of Integza videos for example he builds a cool rocket engine but it melts because, well, plastic and heat are just not a great combination. Again, I get that it might be fine for a proof of concept and allows him to try many more variations in way less time and for less money, but sometimes I just want to see a prototype that lasts for more than first use only.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I agree with you mate - you shouldn't use it for everything - I don't - it might seem that way because i often focus on it but you are 100% right - it's just a tool so you should use it for what it is good at

author: @totherarf
text:
Hysteresis is a funny thing. 
You see it in all sorts of places from moving a magnet to people.
For those not aware hysteresis is most commonly known as an effect in physics. It is the inertia to change. So when you move a magnet you move the field around it and that field interacts with stuff. If the stuff is magnetic in nature it will mimic the pattern of your moving magnet ..... but there is a delay. The same thing can be seen in electronics. You get the famous (to those who studied it) hysteresis curve. This is a graphic of how things are reluctant to change ..... but once they have changed they are reluctant to return to the starting point.

The same thing is true of 3D printers! There is a natural reluctance to try the "New fangled stuff of withchery", but once it is shown to work some try it and the more that like it the more the pressure to move over is. One effect of this hysteresis is the reluctance of people to try a new thing. Now people are smarter than magnets (usually...... possibly with the exception of Politicians) so they recognise this reluctance but want to have a good reason for it (it is called rationalisation) so they come up with excuses, the more plausible the better. One of the more plausible ones is Cost! So that is the one they trot out! It does not have to be right, it just has to feel right to them. One thing to note is that it will be the same people in the future saying "That new way is just to dear/complicated/slow, just stick with the tried and tested 3D printers we all have"

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: very nicely put mate

I've learned a tremendous amount of things from you and you've got my wheels turning many many times. As of late tho I just feel like I can't really relate as much as I used to. author: @pedrojorge4684
text:
Perhaps people did not express their intentions well. In my view, it's not about being expensive a 3d printer, it's about one of the biggest motivations for people to watch your channel is this pleasant feeling of growth in power (which comes through knowledge) but this is linked to him having the power independently, like, "if I want to make a generator I can do it, I have that power now" It's obvious that he could have gone buy a generator on eBay for pennies, but that's not the point.

 The nice thing is to be able to do something cool without depending on the system/society (or depending as little as possible). That's why videos on how to make generators, batteries, etc are so popular that people could simply buy them easily at the store, it's not about being able to buy, but about this empowerment that comes from the knowledge of being able to do something cool without depending on others. Using the 3D printer ends up not being so satisfactory in this sense.

:)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: If they really want empowerment - they should keep up - not sit in the past and whine about it. If they don't keep up all they are doing is constructing a confusing world for themselves where they will be less empowered

replies.author: @pedrojorge4684
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering By logic, yes, but I think it's not about logic, but about the natural self-satisfaction that comes from this feeling of an independent and rustic power. 

That's why a channel with an Indian building a mud house using just a stick gets millions of views (and similar channels).

 I just tried to express what seems to me to be the feeling of part of the viewers who don't like it when the development of things is too dependent on society (tech that has to buy, etc)

 Of course it shouldn't represent everyone's opinion. cheers mate \o/

author: @KronosGodwisen
text:
The price of wood is worth many rants.
If you think wood is expensive don't even think about metal unless you have a scrap yard close at hand.
I was looking around the hobby store the other day and noticed the price of clay and think I could have bought a half dozen ceramic coffee mugs for the price they wanted for a lump if I purchased it there.

I don't have a 3d printer, and it's starting to feel silly not to. Especially since I love doing cad.

I think it's a matter of what medium one likes to work with. There is enormous power in making stuff with computerized designs. There is also enormous power in being able to make things with traditional tools. It's probably the ingenuity of how they are used that is what's most interesting.  

That laser is a monster. It would be hard to justify purchasing it for most people. So I hope to see many projects using it. Sometimes you need see what's possible, then think how can it be made a different say. It looks like a great tool since even it it's easy to one thing by hand, it might get boring to have to do 50 of the exact same thing.

author: @onbedoeldekut1515
text:
Sorry, Bob, but you're mistaken.
As someone who is disabled, everything is expensive.
Regular life expenses have to be multiplied to allow myself the same freedoms that 'able-bodied' people never have to consider, and the 'benefits' I receive are only there to provide those freedoms.
Before I started getting them, life was far more of a struggle, and the prospect of even saving for something as 'cheap' as a printer was a pipe dream at best.
As the welfare payment is every 28 days, every so often, things swing around so that I can splurge a little, knowing I won't have to wait too long on the breadline.
Things like Patreon accounts and the privileges they provide those fortunate enough to afford them feel like taxes on the disabled and unintentionally poor.
I hope you truly appreciate the wealth of health you have, and the ignorant privileges you afford yourself, disregarding the many of us whose lives are often confined to the vicarious indulgences presented to us by those we once respected.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am sorry for your personal circumstances - but to be honest  i don't really mind if you respect me or not - that's your choice not mine.

author: @marcfruchtman9473
text:
100% agree with you. Low cost 3D printers are a great deal. It's definitely an easy way to get started at the creative and hobby level. But sure, LASER cutters are a bit pricey still. However, I don't think they are too costly. Please continue to include them in builds, they produce a fantastic finish and having a CNC tool chain is useful. Mills and Lathes are fairly common machining tools and despite the cost, I consider using those fair game as well. 
However, as ntg03937 commented, don't try to 3d print that can be purchased at a much lower price from a store. 
Also, generally speaking the strength of 3d printed materials is fairly low unless you are very careful with design, or use a fairly expensive material... like the new PPA-CF, which is apparently very strong.

author: @grendel1960a
text:
worstcase - nearly every school will have a 3d printer, get your kid or grandkid to take the file into school and print it for you. I have 3d printers- they dont get used every day, but they do get used regularly, now when I want a part, rather than spending hours making it, I spend a little while drawing it up, and the 3d printer spends hours making it while i am doing something else, or while i am asleep, or busy at work (I work from home which helps)
if i want to make something from wood or metal, I use different tools, but the 3d printer can make new parts for other tools, I wanter 30 brass cable clips- i 3d printed the top and bottom die required, and used that in a small hand press and made my 30 clips, the die is now pretty much shot, but if I want to make more brass clips, I will spend the 30p of materiel and make another- or make a different one that i need this time round, I could have made a permenant die by drilling and filing and grinding a piece of metal, but if I am only wanting 30 clips, plastic is good enough.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: tools for the job mate - nice one

text: You missed what I consider the most important reason to stay away from 3d printers and that is the encouragement of using more plastic rather than less and let's be honest while you may learn lots with making models and gadgets 99.9% of it will be in a dump in 100 years time ... just my rant, but we just don't need all the rubbish we surround ourselves with and 3d printers only serve to increase it

author: @GavinImpett
text: The cost of one commercial wind generator appropriate for someone in living in an urban wind tunnel, AKA me, vs the cost of an Elegoo printer is what sealed the deal for me.  Having reconstructed my office/gym/editing bay/ tv studio to also include a petrol-chemical, tire-burning factory, I will say, I think you are playing a bit fast and loose with the safety issues of 3d printers.  My 3d printer now lives in a sealed box with a Hepa filter. My first printing project was to build a seal on my window for the vent coming from the printer box to the window. I don't know if you have peak hour pricing in the UK, but we do in the US, so a 3d printer running at 480w during peak hours probably costs more on a per second basis than a chop saw running on off-peak hours.  There is also the possibility of burning down the house,  decapitating the neighbor's child(bad) or off-leash dog(good) and so on. So, IMO, the "it's a bit dear," argument isn't just about the money and learning curve.  It's more of a, "is it worth doing, will I look stupid" question. Not everyone is from Kent, you know.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i only ever speak about myself and my experience - it's really all I know about. But then we all do that. I have found Americans overly concerned with safety and I often wonder why. Do you know why they worry so much about it?

replies.author: @GavinImpett

author: @DM-kl4em
text:
My suggestion would be to find items that you can 3D print and sell online, such as on ebay, Etsy, etc.  Then you can use the income to buy additional filament.  

I went with the Christmas/birthday suggestion myself, and bought mine with some birthday money.

I will also say to start with a small and inexpensive, but functional printer, so you can start learning how to 3D print, and different techniques like optimizing material and orienting the print optimally so that supports are easy to remove afterwards.  You can always get a bigger, high-end printer when you get better at it, and you will still have the original small printer to supplement production capacity.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @johang1293
text: You should take a look at FreeCAD now when V1 is about to be released. Both free and open source and you can do anything you want with your files. If you know how to read technical drawings it's very easy to understand and learn the basic sketching. A great alternative to tinkercad. For slicing you can't go wrong with Orca slicer. Again free and open source.

author: @FormerlyKnownAsAndrew
text:
I am a woodworker.... Laser cutters are def cost prohibitive.    Before I even knew about laser cutters, I was making similar stuff using a scroll saw... And before that, by hand using a coping saw.  It can be done.  

You can get a new coping saw for $10.

I bought my scroll saw for $40 Canadian.

replies.author: @FormerlyKnownAsAndrew
replies.text: ...the scroll saw was second hand. People are getting rid of them all the time.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you can certainly do it by hand - my argument is anything done on a laser cutter can be done by hand - there are only two real differences - you don't need the saw skills - and good fretsaw skills are challenge to learn - and you don't need the time

author: @BestplacestocampinAmeric-hm5fg
text: Can I ask a question? Can I use a stationary serpentine coil  with the conductive paint spinning on either side instead of magnets to create no drag electricity?

replies.author: @RobC1999
replies.text: No. Generating electricity with a generator requires pushing EMF so there will always be a drag.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you need a magnetic field - you could create one with your conductive paint but it would be weak even then you would still have 'drag'

I want to make a series of them, set horizontally. Hopefully generating enough to power a house. Ideally without magnets or copper. Is that even possible?

author: @ntg03937
text: I have only been 3d printing for around 8 months or so, but one of the unintended effects that i didn't see coming was that I now look for ways to *avoid* 3d printing, or rather how i can just 3d print a certain aspect of a design to compliment the rest of the design with elements sourced from existing products: your basement, the side of the road, the dollar store. The point here is that being judicious about what you print or don't print can save you lots of time and lots of money. If you want to print your own flower pots, of course you are going to say that 3d printing is expensive, when you can buy an attractive flower pot at the dollar store for a few dollars. Adapt your designs around existing elements and you have the best of both worlds

replies.author: @VeniceInventors
replies.text: So true. I used to systematically 3d print everything I could until I started noticing that some shapes weren't worth printing as injection-molded parts already existed with the shape I needed and generally used better plastic, looking better too. I do like having the ability to model a custom case around a model of some electronic device I'm making, but in most instances there are plenty of shapes and closing options out there with just the right size and tighter tolerances than my printer can print.

author: @frankdutton9852
text: Fusion 360 is totally free if you don't use it in a commercial setting. I believe that there is a limit of £1000 annual sales, but a hobbyist wouldn't reach that. I've used it for free ever since retirement. I design and make 3D printed clocks, and no two are the same.

replies.author: @grendel1960a
replies.text: it does limit you to about 10 active models, but I have never found that an issue, just file some as inactive and when you need them change the status back to active. I have hundreds of designs in fusion (they changed the name to just fusion recently) and have never had an issue opening any one i wanted. fusion is good if you already have autocad experience as the learning curve between them is pretty shallow.

author: @bar1066
text: Its not expensive to buy or build with a 3D printer, the problem seems to be with making an effort to create a 3D model. Once you build something useful it becomes addictive, you will always be looking for the next project. Inertia and the understanding that you may have to make mistakes to learn a new skill are the hardest lessons you will have to overcome. Dont be afraid, give it a go and don't make excuses like "its to expensive".

author: @baldeagle5297
text:
There are a *lot* of things better made with traditional tools. I own an FDM printer, a resin printer, and a laser cutter. I still use my table saw, scroll saw, et al. for a lot of jobs. Sometimes it's quicker *and* cheaper to just bang it on the workbench and send it out in minutes instead of hours.

Don't get me wrong, I love my tech tools. It's really so much easier to build a guitar stand than to print one.
